title: AI, Jobs, and Your Career

modules.title: Separating Automation Myths from Facts
modules.sub: Cutting through the noise with evidence-based automation insights
modules.description: Learners examine the most pervasive AI-and-jobs myths circulating in media and public discourse, then weigh them against current research. By the end, they can confidently distinguish hype from grounded analysis and apply that critical lens to future headlines.

modules.title: Which Tasks AI Automates First
modules.sub: Identifying the specific task types AI targets before entire roles
modules.description: Learners break down jobs into their component tasks and evaluate each against the characteristics that make automation likely — repetition, codifiability, and data availability. They leave with a personal task-level audit framework they can apply to their own role immediately.

modules.title: Jobs Created by the AI Boom
modules.sub: Mapping the new roles and career paths AI is generating
modules.description: Learners explore categories of emerging roles — from AI trainers and prompt specialists to ethics auditors and human-AI workflow designers — and assess where genuine hiring demand is growing. They build a shortlist of opportunity areas aligned with their existing skills and interests.

modules.title: Vulnerable Industries: An Honest Look
modules.sub: Assessing which sectors face the most significant near-term disruption
modules.description: Learners analyze disruption timelines and displacement patterns across industries including logistics, finance, legal services, and customer support, using real data rather than worst-case scenarios. They identify where their own industry sits on the risk spectrum and what signals to watch going forward.

modules.title: Reskilling Strategies That Actually Work
modules.sub: Choosing learning investments that deliver durable career value
modules.description: Learners evaluate reskilling approaches — formal degrees, micro-credentials, on-the-job learning, and peer networks — against evidence of what actually improves employability in an AI-shaped labor market. They draft a prioritized personal learning plan with realistic timelines and resource requirements.

modules.title: AI as Your Workplace Collaborator
modules.sub: Building productive human-AI working relationships in your role
modules.description: Learners practice a collaboration mindset by exploring how to delegate effectively to AI tools, maintain oversight, and reclaim time for higher-judgment work. They design a concrete workflow adjustment for their own job that integrates an AI tool to amplify rather than replace their contribution.

modules.title: Policy Responses Around the World
modules.sub: Surveying how governments and institutions are responding to AI disruption
modules.description: Learners compare policy approaches across regions — including retraining funds, portable benefits, algorithmic accountability laws, and social safety net adaptations — to understand the structural support landscape. They assess how current or likely policies in their context affect their personal career planning.

modules.title: Designing Your AI-Resilient Future
modules.sub: Synthesizing insights into a personalized, actionable career strategy
modules.description: Learners integrate everything from prior modules to produce a written AI-resilience career plan, identifying their highest-value skills, near-term reskilling moves, and a monitoring system for tracking labor market signals. They leave the course with a living document they can revisit and update as the AI landscape continues to evolve.

synopsis: Headlines about AI stealing jobs tell only part of the story — this course gives you the full picture, from which roles are genuinely at risk to the new opportunities emerging in an AI-shaped economy. Walk away with practical strategies for navigating your own career.
rationale: A demand score of 6/10 alongside 0.61 similarity to the adjacent but distinct 'What AI Actually Does to Jobs' signals learner appetite for a broader, action-oriented course that bridges economic analysis with personal career planning.
nearest_existing: What AI Actually Does to Jobs
